claim_summary: Homepage says Kolm compiles data into a model, returns a signed artifact that runs anywhere offline, shows 2.34 GB model artifact, ed25519 signature, model plus LoRA plus draft pack plus index, phone support, public-registry anchoring, and on-chain receipts.
evidence_truth: Local README and source say v0 artifacts are cloud-runtime pointer/recipe artifacts; receipts are HMAC; on-chain and public-key modes are not shipped.
next_action: Replace live homepage with v0-accurate recipe-tier copy or ship real model/LoRA/mobile/public-key receipt proof before restoring these claims.

claim_summary: Live pricing still uses Recipe/REM Labs vocabulary, old package names, savings claims, Specialists included, VPC/on-prem option, SOC 2 Type II Q2 2027, and air-gapped runtime/custom marketplace language.
evidence_truth: Local repo and current docs have moved to Kolm vocabulary; Specialist training is preview/bridge-dependent; enterprise compliance evidence remains roadmap.
next_action: Rebuild live pricing from current PLAN_CATALOG/API copy and remove Recipe/REM Labs drift, unproven specialist/VPC/airgap/SOC2 language, and unsupported packages.

claim_summary: Live docs say 21 endpoints and use @kolmogorov/recipe, kolmogorov-recipe, Railway URLs, and Recipe MCP package names.
evidence_truth: Local router has 86 inline route declarations plus OAuth routes; local SDK package tested as @kolmogorov/kolm-sdk; product brand is Kolm.
next_action: Generate docs from a route manifest or replace live docs with current API reference from repo before launch.

claim_summary: Live device page says the full public registry downloads to phone, every recipe runs locally/offline with no GPU/API bill, and the registry is the model.
evidence_truth: This is plausible for deterministic JS recipe export/PWA behavior, but the language can be read as local model execution. It should be explicitly labeled recipe-registry mode.
next_action: Keep the demo but add a tier label: 'recipe-registry mode, no model weights, no LoRA, no private corpus'.

claim_summary: Local homepage says model/examples/evaluator packaged into one runnable artifact, laptop/phone/VPC, no data leaves, no API, no per-token bill, every run makes it better.
evidence_truth: Current source supports recipe-tier artifacts and compile/run receipts, not general phone/VPC local model execution or automatic learning every run.
next_action: Qualify hero and metadata: recipe-tier today; local model/LoRA/runtime targets are roadmap or enterprise-validated deployments.

claim_summary: API page says compile fits a LoRA, capture/distill trains a LoRA and ships signed artifact, and anyone can re-verify offline against a registry HMAC chain.
evidence_truth: `src/compile.js` marks LoRA as Sprint 3; `src/router.js` auto-distill returns 503 if bridge missing; receipts are HMAC with shared secret, not public-key verification.
next_action: Split current API docs into shipped and preview sections; mark auto-distill and LoRA bridge as preview; define receipt modes honestly.

claim_summary: FAQ says `.kolm` contains base model, personal LoRA, recipe pack, recall index, verifier, manifest, signature chain, and student baked in.
evidence_truth: `src/artifact.js` writes `model.gguf` as pointer metadata and `lora.bin`/`index.sqlite-vec` as v0 slots or behavior/index containers, not real model/LoRA/sqlite-vec by default.
next_action: Rewrite FAQ to reflect artifact tiers: recipe, pointer, adapter/specialist future, bundle future.

claim_summary: Healthcare page metadata says HIPAA-ready offline specialist and PHI never leaves device/network; body also includes a good disclaimer that public product is not a HIPAA compliance certification.
evidence_truth: The disclaimer is good, but SEO metadata and lede still create a compliance/privacy guarantee beyond default hosted compile/capture behavior.
next_action: Make metadata and hero as careful as the disclaimer: 'Enterprise design target' and 'customer-controlled deployment required for PHI'.

claim_summary: BAA page says standard BAA/DPA template, founder-signed within 48 hours, PHI never leaves covered entity network at runtime, and subprocessor list details.
evidence_truth: May be useful for enterprise sales but it is legal/compliance content. It needs owner review and current subprocessor evidence before public reliance.
next_action: Add legal-owner status, last-reviewed date, and 'draft template, not executed agreement' guardrails in metadata and page top.

claim_summary: Mobile page claims Hermes-class phone behavior, iPhone/Pixel support, 35-90 tok/s, React Native/Swift/Kotlin SDK, llama.cpp and ExecuTorch, and offline signed inference.
evidence_truth: Line 239 admits RN module is preview and today is browser-WASM via WebView. The rest reads as shipped device runtime and benchmark data.
next_action: Move performance table and SDK claims to roadmap or 'target' until reproducible device benchmarks and SDK packages exist.

claim_summary: Device page says public registry recipes run locally/offline via PWA and service worker.
evidence_truth: This is the safest local/offline claim if scoped to public JS recipes from `/v1/registry/export`. It should avoid implying model weights.
next_action: Use this page as the model for safe 'offline' language: recipe-registry mode, not full specialist runtime.

claim_summary: Benchmarks page focuses on embedded evals, offline recipe-tier benchmark, monitored egress, HMAC chain, and fixture secret.
evidence_truth: This aligns with artifact-end-to-end tests and is comparatively honest, though HMAC/shared-secret limitations should remain visible.
next_action: Keep benchmark page as proof anchor; link from overclaiming pages as the current shipped evidence.

claim_summary: Build-your-own page makes safe HMAC/local-secret claims, but later says auto-distill can output recipe or LoRA and offline specialist.
evidence_truth: Local spec compile is real for recipe-tier; auto-distill/LoRA remains bridge-dependent.
next_action: Split local authoring recipe-tier proof from capture/auto-distill preview copy.

claim_summary: Article explicitly says current public product proves recipe-mode artifact path and full native model execution requires runtime bridge work and benchmarks.
evidence_truth: This is the clearest safe wording for public long-form claims.
next_action: Reuse this qualification language across homepage, FAQ, API, healthcare, and mobile pages.

claim_summary: Article has a step that says compiler produces LoRA delta and recall index, but later clearly says native weights, LoRA adapters, and sqlite-vec indexes require runtime bridge and device benchmarks.
evidence_truth: Good caution appears after a stronger step claim. Readers may stop at the stronger claim.
next_action: Revise Step 3 to say target architecture, and make current product limitation visible before the workflow steps.

claim_summary: Site test already encodes forbidden claims: HIPAA-ready, EU AI Act compliant, on-chain receipt anchoring, mobile SDK, zero runtime egress, inside your VPC, fully self-contained, HMAC chain to registry, and stale Recipe branding.
evidence_truth: This is valuable claim governance, but it only protects deploys if run and if live pages match local files.
next_action: Add a live-site claim audit or deploy check that fetches key live pages and applies the same forbidden-pattern set.
